name: Juzumaru Tsunetsugu
description: Juzumaru Tsunetsugu is one of the Five Great Swords of Japan (Tenka Goken), renowned for its exquisite craftsmanship and spiritual significance. Forged by the legendary swordsmith Tsunetsugu during the Kamakura period, its name, meaning "Rosary Beads," is derived from the rosary beads that were once attached to the sword's hilt. These beads were added by the monk Nichiren, who possessed the sword for a time, hoping to pacify its violent spirit and use it to spread Buddhist teachings. The sword is said to possess the power to ward off evil spirits and bring peace to those who wield it. Its blade is believed to be imbued with the spirit of Nichiren, granting it a protective aura. The Juzumaru is not merely a weapon but a symbol of spiritual purification and the power of faith to overcome darkness. Its elegant curvature and flawless polish reflect the dedication and artistry of its creator. It is a national treasure of Japan, carefully preserved and rarely displayed, representing a profound connection to the nation's spiritual and historical heritage. The sword serves as a reminder of the complex interplay between violence, faith, and the pursuit of enlightenment in Japanese culture.
subtitle: The Rosary Sword of Spiritual Power
origin: Forged by Tsunetsugu during the Kamakura period, later owned by Nichiren.

keyMyths.title: Nichiren's Pacification
keyMyths.description: The monk Nichiren acquired Juzumaru and attached rosary beads to its hilt, hoping to quell its violent nature and use it for good. This act transformed the sword from a mere weapon into a symbol of spiritual power.
keyMyths.source: Various historical accounts and temple records

keyMyths.title: One of the Five Great Swords
keyMyths.description: Juzumaru is celebrated as one of the Tenka Goken, the Five Great Swords of Japan, renowned for their exceptional quality and historical significance. This distinction solidified its place as a national treasure.
keyMyths.source: Japanese sword lore and historical texts

sources.author: Kanzan Sato
sources.work: The Japanese Sword: A Comprehensive Guide
sources.description: Provides detailed information on the history and craftsmanship of Japanese swords, including the Tenka Goken.
sources.text: Provides detailed information on the history and craftsmanship of Japanese swords, including the Tenka Goken.

sources.author: Honkō-ji Temple
sources.description: Contains historical accounts and information about the sword's preservation and significance within the temple.
sources.text: Contains historical accounts and information about the sword's preservation and significance within the temple.

worshipPractices.name: Sword Consecration
worshipPractices.description: Swords like Juzumaru Tsunetsugu are sometimes consecrated in Shinto shrines to purify them and imbue them with spiritual power. This involves rituals performed by priests, offering prayers for the sword's protection and the well-being of its owner or the community it represents.

worshipPractices.name: Display and Appreciation
worshipPractices.description: Rather than direct worship, swords of Juzumaru's caliber are often treated with reverence and displayed as objects of cultural and artistic significance. Their preservation and proper handling are seen as important ways to honor the craftsmanship and history they represent.

festivals.name: Token Ranbu Festivals
festivals.description: Due to the popularity of the character based on the sword in 'Touken Ranbu', fans participate in events centered around the game and its characters, often visiting locations associated with the swords or commissioning art. These events serve as modern expressions of appreciation for the historical and cultural significance of the swords.

cultural.modernLegacy: Juzumaru Tsunetsugu's modern legacy is primarily maintained through its inclusion in popular media, particularly the online game 'Touken Ranbu', where it is depicted as an anthropomorphic sword spirit. This has significantly increased awareness of the sword and its historical context, sparking interest in Japanese swordmaking and mythology among younger generations and global audiences.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Juzumaru and Gungnir are iconic weapons imbued with significant cultural and spiritual power, held by important figures and representing authority/skill.
Both Excalibur and Juzumaru are legendary swords associated with exceptional craftsmanship and a rich history, representing power and prestige within their respective cultures.
Though a shield, the Aegis, like Juzumaru, represents a powerful artifact imbued with protective qualities and a spiritual significance, both being tied to important figures/deities.

associations.name: Pacification
associations.significance: The rosary beads wrapped around the sword suggest a calming and pacifying influence, contrasting with its sharp, deadly nature.

associations.name: Five
associations.significance: Juzumaru is one of the 'Tenka Goken', the Five Great Swords, representing a pinnacle of swordsmithing and cultural importance.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Water represents clarity and adaptability, reflecting the sword's sharp edge and the wielder's ability to navigate complex situations with serenity.

associations.name: Lotus
associations.significance: The lotus symbolizes purity and enlightenment, aligning with the sword's association with Buddhist rosary beads and spiritual significance.

associations.name: Indigo
associations.significance: Indigo represents a blend of calmness and strength, mirroring the sword's spiritual tranquility and martial prowess.

associations.name: The Moon
associations.significance: The moon represents serenity and reflection, mirroring the sword's association with rosary beads and introspective nature.

associations.name: Balance
associations.significance: Juzumaru embodies the balance between martial skill and spiritual restraint, represented by the sword and rosary beads respectively.

companions.name: Tsunetsugu
companions.description: Tsunetsugu was the legendary swordsmith who forged Juzumaru. His skill and dedication imbued the sword with its exceptional quality and spiritual properties.

companions.name: Nichiren
companions.description: Juzumaru was once owned by the Buddhist monk Nichiren. He is said to have carried the sword as protection during his travels and teachings.

companions.name: Other Tenka Goken Swords
companions.description: As one of the Five Great Swords of Japan, Juzumaru shares a connection with the other legendary blades. They are often considered to be kindred spirits, representing the pinnacle of Japanese swordsmithing.

companions.name: Fudo Myoo
companions.description: Fudo Myoo is a fierce protector deity in Buddhism, and the rosary beads associated with Juzumaru are often linked to Fudo Myoo's symbolism. The sword may be seen as channeling Fudo Myoo's protective power.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Craftsmanship
extendedContent.content: Juzumaru Tsunetsugu stands as a testament to the exceptional skill of Japanese swordsmiths during the Kamakura period. Created by Tsunetsugu, a master craftsman of the era, the sword exemplifies the refined aesthetics and technical prowess characteristic of the time. The blade is known for its elegant curvature, a hallmark of Japanese swords, and its exceptional sharpness, a crucial attribute for its intended purpose as a weapon. The forging process involved meticulous folding and hammering of steel to create a blade of unparalleled strength and flexibility. Its creation reflects a deep understanding of metallurgy and the art of sword making, elevating it beyond a mere weapon to a work of art. The sword's intricate details, such as the hamon (temper line), further enhance its visual appeal and attest to the smith's dedication to perfection.

extendedContent.title: The Significance of the Rosary Beads
extendedContent.content: The name "Juzumaru," meaning "Rosary Beads," is directly linked to the sword's association with the Buddhist monk Nichiren. According to legend, Nichiren, a prominent figure in Japanese Buddhism, possessed the sword and attached rosary beads to its hilt. This act transformed the weapon into a symbol of spiritual protection and purification. The rosary beads were believed to ward off evil spirits and bring peace to the wielder. This association significantly elevated the sword's status beyond that of a mere weapon. It became a sacred object, imbued with religious significance and revered for its perceived ability to provide spiritual defense. The rosary beads served as a constant reminder of the Buddhist principles of compassion and non-violence, contrasting with the sword's inherent purpose as an instrument of war.

extendedContent.title: Historical Provenance and Legacy
extendedContent.content: Throughout its history, Juzumaru Tsunetsugu has been passed down through the hands of various powerful figures, adding to its mystique and prestige. Its presence in the possession of significant historical figures further solidifies its place as a national treasure. The sword's journey through different eras of Japanese history reflects the changing political landscape and the importance placed on martial prowess. Today, Juzumaru Tsunetsugu is carefully preserved and displayed as a cultural artifact, representing the pinnacle of Japanese swordsmithing and its rich historical and spiritual heritage. It serves as a tangible link to the past, allowing modern generations to appreciate the artistry and craftsmanship of the Kamakura period and the enduring legacy of Japanese sword culture.

shortDescription: One of Japan's Five Great Swords (Tenka Goken), forged by Tsunetsugu. Named "Rosary Beads" for the beads added by Nichiren, it's a symbol of craftsmanship and spirit.

wielders.name: Nichiren
wielders.context: Buddhist monk who added rosary beads.

wielders.name: Various Samurai Lords
wielders.context: Passed down through powerful samurai families
